coredata.dc:title: L-arginine and 1,4-Butanediol conjugated highly ordered nitrogen doped carbon dots as photoluminescent probe for “turn off” based detection of organic pollutants in real sample matrices
coredata.dc:creator: Bisht, Krishanan
Dutta, R.K.
coredata.dc:description: Nitrogen doped carbon dots are emerging as efficient photoluminescent probes for wide range of sensing applications. A simple strategy of synthesizing highly ordered nitrogen doped carbon dots (named here as N-CDarg) is developed by thermal conjugation of 1, 4-Butanediol with L-Arginine. It led to formation of self-assembled transverse carbon chains through oligomerization of 1,4-Butanediol and conjugation with L-Arginine. The structural, morphological and compositional analysis of the N-CDarg is confirmed from 1H and 13C NMR, mass spectroscopy, FT-IR, higher resolution transmission electron microscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. It exhibited bright bluish photoluminescence with PL quantum yield of 11 %. Under optimized pH and contact time, the probe solution of N-CDarg exhibited dual sensing for the detection of tetracycline (TC) and 4-nitrophenol (4-NP) via PL quenching proportional, which followed Stern-Volmer relation. The linear PL quenching response for TC is in the range of 0.5 μM to 60 μM with a limit of detection (LOD) of 103 nM; while the linear range for 4-NP is 0.4 μM to 20 μM, and the LOD is 77 nM. Selectivity of these species have been confirmed against several metal ions, anions, other antibiotics (for TC detection) and different structural analogues of phenols (for 4-NP detection). The ability of the probe to quantitatively detect trace concentrations of TC and 4-NP in real sample matrices like river water and milk has been demonstrated by spike analysis. The mechanism of PL quenching of N-CDarg by TC and 4-NP has been discussed using PL decay lifetime spectroscopy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots
Photoluminescence quenching
Tetracycline and 4-nitrophenol detection
PL lifetime and detection mechanism
Spike analysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen doped carbon dots

xocs:item-toc-section-title.ce:bold: Analytical method and optimization for detection of TC and 4-NP using N-CD

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Spike analysis of TC in milk samples
Characterization of the N-CDarg and optical properties
PL quenching based detection of TC and 4-NP

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: KB is grateful to IIT Roorkee for providing a Senior Research Fellowship. The authors acknowledge DST-FIST, New Delhi (SR/FST/CS-II/2018/72) for funding 500 MHz NMR facility in the Department of Chemistry, IIT Roorkee . The authors are thankful to Prof. B.P. Vellanki, Civil Engineering Department of IIT Roorkee for MS-QToF facility. The authors also acknowledge Institute Instrumentation Centre of IIT Roorkee for the XPS and FLS facility, and Department of Metallurgical and Materials Engineering , IIT Roorkee for 300 kV HRTEM facility.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Transmission electron microscopy image (TEM); (b) particle size distribution plot; (c) HRTEM image with lattice fringes; and (d) FT-IR spectrum of the synthesized N-CDarg.
X-ray photoelectron spectra showing (a) survey scan; detailed binding energy spectra of (b) C 1s, (c) O 1s, and (d) N 1s of N-CDarg.
(a) UV−Vis, (b) PL spectra with the inset showing bluish PL emission under UV light of 365 nm; (c) excitation dependent emission spectrum; and (d) PL lifetime spectrum of N-CDarg.

(a) Shows decrease in the PL intensity of N-CDarg with increase of TC (μM) ranging between 0.50 μM and 200 μM; (b) Stern-Volmer plot of N-CDarg treated with different concentrations of TC; Inset: linear part of Stern − Volmer quenching profile for N-CDarg treated with TC concentration ranging between 0.50 μM and 60.00 μM; (c) PL spectra of N-CDarg treated with 4-NP ranging between 0.40 μM and 200 μM (d) Stern − Volmer plot of N-CDarg treated with different concentrations of 4-NP ranging between 0.40 and 200 μM; inset linear stern volmer plot ranging from 0.40 μM and 20 μM.

PL spectra of the N-CDarg treated with interfering (a) antibiotics i.e. ciprofloxacin (CIP), norfloxacin (NOR), penicillin (PEN), chloramphenicol (CHL), amoxicillin (AMX), erythromycin (ERY), and oxytetracycline (OTC); (b) cations; (c) anions and (d) structural analogues of 4-nitrophenol, e.g., 3-Nitrotoulene (3 NT), 4-Chloroaniline (4 CA), 2-Nitroaniline (2NA), 4-Nitrobenzoic acid (4 NBA), 2-Chlorophenol (2 CP), 1,3-Dinitrobenzene (1,3 DNB), 4-Aniline (4NA), 2,4-Dinitrophenol (DNP) and 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (TNP).

(a) Stern-Volmer plot for N-CDarg treated with TC spiked deionized water, municipality tap water and urban river water samples; (b) Stern-Volmer plot for N-CDarg treated with 4-NP spiked deionized water, municipality tap water and urban river water samples; (c) Stern-Volmer plot for the probe with TC spiked milk samples by treatment with TCA.
Schematic representation of the synthesis of N–CDarg.
PL quenching of N-CDarg by TC and 4-NP, the average PL decay lifetime of probe treated with TC as well as 4-NP are same as that of the probe indicating static quenching.
Individual photoluminescence lifetime parameters (τ1, τ2 and τ3) and τavg value of probe (N-CDarg), probe in presence of 10 μM, 30 μM, 50 μM, and 100 μM of Tetracycline.
Individual photoluminescence lifetime parameters (τ1, τ2 and τ3) and τavg of probe N-CDarg in presence of 10 μM, 30 μM, 50 μM, and 100 μM of 4-nitrophenol.

head.ce:title: L-arginine and 1,4-Butanediol conjugated highly ordered nitrogen doped carbon dots as photoluminescent probe for “turn off” based detection of organic pollutants in real sample matrices

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology, Roorkee 247667, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology, Roorkee 247667, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen doped carbon dots are emerging as efficient photoluminescent probes for wide range of sensing applications. A simple strategy of synthesizing highly ordered nitrogen doped carbon dots (named here as N-CDarg) is developed by thermal conjugation of 1, 4-Butanediol with L-Arginine. It led to formation of self-assembled transverse carbon chains through oligomerization of 1,4-Butanediol and conjugation with L-Arginine. The structural, morphological and compositional analysis of the N-CDarg is confirmed from 1H and 13C NMR, mass spectroscopy, FT-IR, higher resolution transmission electron microscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. It exhibited bright bluish photoluminescence with PL quantum yield of 11 %. Under optimized pH and contact time, the probe solution of N-CDarg exhibited dual sensing for the detection of tetracycline (TC) and 4-nitrophenol (4-NP) via PL quenching proportional, which followed Stern-Volmer relation. The linear PL quenching response for TC is in the range of 0.5 μM to 60 μM with a limit of detection (LOD) of 103 nM; while the linear range for 4-NP is 0.4 μM to 20 μM, and the LOD is 77 nM. Selectivity of these species have been confirmed against several metal ions, anions, other antibiotics (for TC detection) and different structural analogues of phenols (for 4-NP detection). The ability of the probe to quantitatively detect trace concentrations of TC and 4-NP in real sample matrices like river water and milk has been demonstrated by spike analysis. The mechanism of PL quenching of N-CDarg by TC and 4-NP has been discussed using PL decay lifetime spectroscopy.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Materials that emit photoluminescence is emerging as an efficient optical probe for wide range of sensing applications [1,2]. It offers highly sensitive, accurate and easy to operate sensing platform where change in the photoluminescence intensity of the probe due to interaction with an analyte is used as an analytical information for sensing [3]. The photoluminescence quenching is quantitatively related to the concentration of the quencher, which is an analyte and follows the Stern-Volmer relation [4]. The limitations of organic molecules as fluoroprobes, e.g., hydrophobic property, photobleaching and influence of fluorescence by external agents are overcome by hydrophilic photoluminescent quantum dots [5–7]. In this regard, water soluble heteroatom doped carbon dots of sizes less than 10 nm exhibiting tunable bright bluish photoluminescence are gaining importance as optical probes for sensing applications [8–11]. Moreover, the heteroatom doped carbon dots are easy to synthesize, are chemically inert and exhibit good photostability. The introduction of heteroatoms into the carbon dots structure induces electron polarization owing to different electronegativity of the dopant than that of carbon and enhances the photoluminescent (PL) properties of heteroatom doped carbon dots [12]. Among several heteroatoms, nitrogen has been efficient owing to its similar atomic size to carbon and ability to change the electrical and optical properties of carbon dots [13]. The optical property depends on the core structure of the carbon dots, which is broadly categorized as carbon skeletal structure comprising sp 2 carbons or mixed sp 2/ sp 3 carbon [14]. The PL property of heteroatom doped carbon dots are primarily attributed to four mechanisms, e.g., (i) the quantum confinement effect (QCE) due to conjugated π-domain [15]; (ii) surface state mechanism contributed by hybridization of the carbon backbone with the attached functional groups [16]; (iii) the molecular state mechanism involving integration of fluorescent molecules within the carbon dots [17]; and (iv) the cross-linking enhanced emission (CEE) effect [18]. Lately, it has been demonstrated that the PL property of heteroatom doped carbon dots is enhanced by improving the crystalline nature of carbon dots [19]. It favors delocalization of charge carriers and assist in improved interaction with surrounding analyte, which is necessary for sensing application [20].

Various methods have been proposed for synthesizing wide range of nitrogen doped carbon dots [21–23]. In this regard, we present here a simple thermal conjugation method for synthesizing highly crystalline nitrogen doped carbon dots by oligomerization of 1,4-Butanediol and conjugation with L-Arginine monohydrochloride, referred to as N-CDarg, as PL probe. The N-CDarg has been characterized and its ability towards quantitative detection of emerging contaminants, e.g., tetracycline and 4-nitrophenol by PL quenching has been studied. The PL quenching mechanism has been addressed by comparing the PL lifetime studies of N-CDarg and those of N-CDarg treated with TC and 4-NP respectively. The laboratory optimized PL quenching method of selectively detecting tetracycline and 4-nitrophenol has been translated to their detection for real samples by spike analysis in municipality tap water, urban river water and in milk samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen doped carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: All the precursors used in this study are of analytical grade and used without further purification (details are given in Section S1, in Supporting Information) The nitrogen doped carbon dots are synthesized by thermal conjugation method, where 0.6 g of L-Arginine monohydrochloride is mixed with 20 mL 1,4-Butanediol in a round bottom flask and heated in an oil bath at 180 ⁰C under constant magnetic stirring. The heating time is varied from 5 min to 180 min. Maximum PL intensity is obtained for the batch heated at 180 ⁰C for 120 min (Fig. S1a, Supporting Information). During the progress of the synthesis, the white color L-Arginine monohydrochloride dispersed in 1,4-Butanediol solution first became a transparent colorless solution and finally turned into a transparent faint yellowish solution. The synthesis method is graphically presented in Scheme 1 . Upon cooling, the yellowish solution exhibited bright bluish photoluminescence when excited at a wavelength of 365 nm in an UV chamber (shown in Scheme 1).

N-CDarg has been characterized by 1H and 13C NMR, MS-QToF, HRTEM, FT-IR, XPS measurement, UV–vis absorption and photoluminescence (PL) spectra to elucidate its structure, morphology, composition and optical properties. The details of these techniques are provided in Supporting Information (Section S2). The quantum yield of N-CDarg has been determined by relative method using Quinine sulfate as standard reference. Details of the quantum yield measurement is given in Supporting Information (Section S3).

ce:section-title.ce:bold: Analytical method and optimization for detection of TC and 4-NP using N-CD

ce:section.ce:para: The detection of TC and 4-NP has been carried out using photoluminescence quenching principle. The analytical method has been optimized with respect to pH of the probe solution (N-CDarg), and the contact time between the probe and analyte. Though the maximum PL intensity is obtained for the probe solution at pH 6–7 (Fig. S1b, given as Supporting Information). and all subsequent experiments are done using probe solution at pH 7. For tetracycline detection, the maximum quenching is observed at pH 6 (Fig. S1c), equilibrated for 8 min (Fig. S1d). Different concentrations of TC solutions ranging between 0.5 μM to 200 μM are prepared by serial dilution of 1 mM standard stock solution of TC in deionized water, conditioned at pH 6. For 4-NP detection, the maximum PL quenching is observed when 4-NP is conditioned at pH 8 (Fig. S1e) and equilibrated for 10 min (Fig. S1f). Different concentrations of 4-NP ranging from 0.4 μM to 150 μM are prepared by serial dilution of 1 mM of the stock solution of 4-NP in deionized water, conditioned at pH 8. The optimum PL quenching is found when 4 mL of TC or 4-NP solution is mixed with 100 μL of optimized probe solution equilibrated for 8 min and 10 min, respectively. The effect of interfering agents on the PL quenching of N-CDarg is studied by treating trace concentrations of cations (e.g., Cu2+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Co2+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Cr3+, Fe2, Mg2+), anions (e.g. Cl-, F-, HCO3 –, NO3 –, SO4 2-, CO3 2–, CH3COO–, AsO4 3-), structural analogues of nitrophenol (e.g., 3-Nitrotoulene, 4-Chloroaniline, 2-Nitroaniline, 4-Nitrobenzoic acid, 2-Chlorophenol, 1,3-Dinitrobenzene, 4-Aniline, 2,4-Dinitrophenol and 2,4,6-trinitrophenol), and common antibiotics (e.g. ciprofloxacin, norfloxacin, penicillin, chloramphenicol, amoxicillin, erythromycin, and oxytetracycline). The interfering agents are prepared by serial dilution of 1 mM respective stock solution by maintaining the optimum pH and contact time.

Though the maximum PL intensity is obtained for the probe solution at pH 6–7 (Fig. S1b, given as Supporting Information). and all subsequent experiments are done using probe solution at pH 7. For tetracycline detection, the maximum quenching is observed at pH 6 (Fig. S1c), equilibrated for 8 min (Fig. S1d). Different concentrations of TC solutions ranging between 0.5 μM to 200 μM are prepared by serial dilution of 1 mM standard stock solution of TC in deionized water, conditioned at pH 6. For 4-NP detection, the maximum PL quenching is observed when 4-NP is conditioned at pH 8 (Fig. S1e) and equilibrated for 10 min (Fig. S1f). Different concentrations of 4-NP ranging from 0.4 μM to 150 μM are prepared by serial dilution of 1 mM of the stock solution of 4-NP in deionized water, conditioned at pH 8. The optimum PL quenching is found when 4 mL of TC or 4-NP solution is mixed with 100 μL of optimized probe solution equilibrated for 8 min and 10 min, respectively. The effect of interfering agents on the PL quenching of N-CDarg is studied by treating trace concentrations of cations (e.g., Cu2+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Co2+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Cr3+, Fe2, Mg2+), anions (e.g. Cl-, F-, HCO3 –, NO3 –, SO4 2-, CO3 2–, CH3COO–, AsO4 3-), structural analogues of nitrophenol (e.g., 3-Nitrotoulene, 4-Chloroaniline, 2-Nitroaniline, 4-Nitrobenzoic acid, 2-Chlorophenol, 1,3-Dinitrobenzene, 4-Aniline, 2,4-Dinitrophenol and 2,4,6-trinitrophenol), and common antibiotics (e.g. ciprofloxacin, norfloxacin, penicillin, chloramphenicol, amoxicillin, erythromycin, and oxytetracycline). The interfering agents are prepared by serial dilution of 1 mM respective stock solution by maintaining the optimum pH and contact time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spike analysis of TC in milk samples
ce:section.ce:para: Batches of 20 mL of the diluted milk sample is spiked by TC solution of concentration ranging between 1 μM and 60 μM and homogenized by magnetic stirring for 30 min. Then 2 g of trichloroacetic acid (TCA) is added to precipitate the proteins, fats and other organic substances present in the spiked milk sample and sonicated for 30 min. The precipitate is separated by centrifuging the above at 15,000 rpm for 6 min. The supernatant liquid is recovered by vacuum filtering through a 0.22 μm (Millipore) membrane to remove any particulate suspension. Batches of the filtrate are then treated with the optimized volume of N-CDarg probe and their PL are recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the N-CDarg and optical properties
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of sub-10 nm irregularly spherical shaped N-CDarg is revealed from the transmission electron microscopy image (Fig. 1 a). A particle size distribution histogram is obtained from the TEM images, which revealed an average diameter of 7.2 ± 2.4 nm (Fig. 1b). The nanocrystalline nature of N-CDarg is reflected from the fringes in the higher-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images (Fig. 1c). The spacing between the fringes is estimated to be 0.21 nm, which indicates ordered internal structure in carbon dots [24,25]. In general, carbon dots with ordered structure is attributable to regular arrangement of aromatic or aliphatic carbons or via cyclisation of precursor materials [26]. In the case of N-CDarg, the ordered structure is due to self-assembly of transverse carbon chains comprising of oligomers of 1,4-Butanediol conjugated with L-arginine. It corresponded to formation of a three dimensionally ordered carbon dots doped with nitrogen group as shown in Scheme-S1 (Supporting Information). The proposed structure of N-CDarg is corroborated by structural elucidation via 1H NMR, 13C NMR and mass spectrometry (Fig. S2, S3, S4, and discussed in Section S4, given in Supporting Information). The possible oligomer structures are given in Table S1 (Supporting Information).

The composition of the carbon dot is studied by FT-IR and XPS. The FT-IR measurement indicates relevant peaks corresponding to the functional groups in N-CDarg (Fig. 1d). The broad band with a peak at 3346 cm−1 is attributed to hydrogen bonding in N-CDarg due to the carboxylic acid group and N–H groups in the L-arginine component. The two peaks at 2945 cm−1 and 2875 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching frequency of C–H bond in the methylene group of L-Arginine and those in the oligomer of 1,4-Butanediol. The weak peak at 2129 cm−1 is due to stretching vibration of imine group (C = N) in L-arginine fraction. The broad peak at 1657 cm−1 is attributable to stretching frequency of –C = O in amide group formed due to conjugation of L-Arginine with 1,4-Butanediol and due to bending frequency of N–H group. In addition to the amide group the peak at 1657 cm−1 is ascribed to C = C stretching in N-CDarg , owing to the terminal alkene group (as shown in Scheme-S1, Supporting Information). The peak at 1432 cm−1 is assigned to bending vibration frequency of C–H in methylene group and due to O–H of carboxylic acid group. The peaks in the region 1200 and 1000 cm−1 are attributable to C-O group of alkyl ether (–CH2-O-CH2- in Scheme-S1, Supporting Information) and C-N group of amines. The proposed terminal alkene group shown in Scheme S1 (Supporting Information) is confirmed form the peak at 950 cm−1 corresponding to C = C bending frequency. The peak at 668 cm−1 is attributable to O–H (out of plane) bending. Overall, the FT-IR studies strongly indicates synthesis of nitrogen doped carbon dots where L-Arginine is conjugated with the oligomer of 1,4-Butanediol.

The composition of N-CDarg is further investigated by XPS. The survey spectrum shows prominent photoelectron lines of the constituent elements, e.g., O 1s, N 1s, and C 1s, at 532 eV, 400 eV, and at 285 eV, respectively (Fig. 2 a). The associated Auger electron lines are also observed. The relative atomic proportions were obtained to be C (67.09 %), O (21.59 %) and N (11.32 %). The occurrence of N 1 s (400 eV) peak with a C: O: N ratio of 6: 2: 1 confirms the existence of nitrogen doping in N-CDarg. The chemical states of C, N, O in N-CDarg are further investigated from their respective detailed binding energy spectra. The C 1s core electron binding energy spectrum revealed a broad higher intensity peak (Fig. 2b). The broad peak is best fitted by de-convulating three distinct peaks at 284.6 eV, 285.3 eV, and 288.8 eV, corresponding to hydrocarbon (e.g., C–C and C–H), carbon bound to nitrogen (e.g., C–N, C = N) and carbon bound to oxygen (e.g. C = O and C-O-C) group, respectively. A weak peak at higher energy (i.e., 288.8 eV) is attributable to C–C-O group. Comparing with literature, these core electron binding energy peaks mostly correspond to those of L-Arginine [27]. Similarly, the O1 s core electron binding energy spectrum revealed a broad peak which is de-convoluted into two peaks at 532.2 and 533.9 eV (Fig. 2c). These peaks are attributable to O = C-O and C-O-C groups, respectively. The N 1s core electron binding energy spectrum (Fig. 2d) revealed a broad peak ranging between 396.4 and 398.2 eV. The possible peaks corresponding to the lower binding energy of C-NH2 while the higher binding energy peak of N 1s is attributable to C = N group of L-arginine. The XPS results corroborated with the FT-IR results and indicated synthesis of L-Arginine conjugated network structure of carbon dots formed by oligomerization of 1,4-Butanediol.

The UV–vis and photoluminescence spectra of N-CDarg are given in Fig. 3 a and 3b, respectively. The UV–vis spectrum shows absorbance shoulders at wavelengths of 276 nm to 312 nm. These are attributable to π–π* transitions originating from double bonds, e.g., terminal alkene group of the oligomer of 1,4-Butanediol, and that of C = O of the amide group of the L-Arginine moeity. In addition, a low intensity tailing of the UV–vis spectrum is observed in the wavelength region of 340–410 nm. This is attributable to the n–π* transition originating from the amide group in N-CDarg. The N-CDarg exhibited bluish emission in a UV chamber illuminated at λ = 365 nm, (given in the inset of Fig. 3). It exhibited excitation wavelength (λex) dependent photoluminescence (PL) spectra of N-CDarg, when recorded in the wavelength range of 320 nm to 380 nm (Fig. 3c). The corresponding PL peaks are systematically shifted from 410 nm to 450 nm. A saddle point showing maximum PL intensity is obtained at an emission wavelength (λem) of 430 nm, corresponding to both λex = 350 nm and 360 nm (Fig. 3c). The excitation wavelength dependent PL property of N-CDarg attributed to multiple emission sites, defects, and heterogeneous microstructure of the nitrogen doped carbon dots [28]. The photoluminescent property of N-CDarg is reflected from relative quantum yield (QY) of 11.0 % measured at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm. The measured QY is though less than those reported for certain types of carbon dots [29], but the QY of N-CDarg corresponds to sufficient light emitting efficiency and it is suitable for optical sensing [30]. The QY of carbon dots depends on various factors, e.g. choice of precursors, surface passivation, size and structural defects, and multiple emission centers [31].

The low photoluminescent quantum yield of N-CDarg is explored from photoluminescence lifetime spectroscopy. The photoluminescence lifetime spectrum given in Fig. 3d, is best fitted with tri-exponential function corresponding to Y(t) = A1exp(-t/τ1) + A2exp(-t/τ2) + A3exp(-t/τ3); where, Y(t) is normalized PL intensity as a function of time; τ1 , τ2 and τ3 , are the time resolved decay lifetime of three radiative decay channels; A1 , A2 and A3 are the corresponding amplitudes. The decay lifetimes and their corresponding amplitudes are listed in the inset of Fig. 3d. The average lifetime of N-CDarg is calculated as 7.8 ns, obtained using the following expression [32].

<
τ a v g
> =
∑
i = 1
3
A ι τ ι 2
∑
i = 1
3
A ι τ ι
.

The tri-exponential behaviour of N-CDarg implies presence of three types of emissive centres that correspond to the internal carbon core and external surface traps. The shorter lifetime component (τ1 ) is less than 1 ns and it is attributable to the direct e-h recombination, whereas the relatively longer lifetime (τ2 and τ3 ) are likely to be due to emission from defect states of N-CDarg [33]. The longer average lifetime, due to significantly higherτ2 and τ3 is responsible for low PLQY of N-CDarg.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PL quenching based detection of TC and 4-NP
ce:section.ce:para: Aqueous solutions of tetracycline hydrochloride and 4-nitrophenol exhibited PL quenching of N-CDarg (shown in Scheme 2 ). The PL spectra of N-CDarg mixed in the batches of solutions of TC concentration ranging between 0.50 μM and 200 μM are clustered in Fig. 4 a. The concave up PL quenching plot of Io/I versus the above TC concentration is obtained (Fig. 4b). A linear response is obtained for TC concentration ranging between 0.50 μM to 60 μM (inset of Fig. 4b) and satisfies the Stern-Volmer equation given as: Io/I = 1 + K sv [TC]. The slope of the linearly fitted plot (i.e., K sv) is determined as 0.030 μM−1, which correspond to the sensitivity of TC detection. The limit of TC detection is determined as 103 nM, calculated using the formula 3σ/K sv. Here σ is the standard deviation of the blank solution of 5 measurements. Similarly, the PL spectra of N-CDarg spiked in the batches of 4-NP concentration ranging between 0.4 and 200 μM at pH 8 is clustered in Fig. 4c. The PL quenching plot of Io/I versus the above batches of 4-NP concentration is given in Fig. 4d. A linear response is obtained for 4-NP concentration ranging between 0.40 μM to 20 μM (inset of Fig. 4d) and satisfies the Stern-Volmer equation. The sensitivity of 4-NP detection by N-CDarg is determined as 0.041 μM−1, while the limit of 4-NP detection is calculated as 77 nM. Though both TC and 4-NP can be detected by N-CDarg but, the probe is sensitive towards detection of 4-NP than TC.

The selectivity of TC detection is investigated against the commonly used antibiotics, ciprofloxacin (CIP), norfloxacin (NOR), penicillin (PEN), chloramphenicol (CHL), amoxicillin (AMX), erythromycin (ERY), and oxytetracycline (OTC) as potential interfering agents. In this study, 60 μM of TC and the respective interfering antibiotics prepared in deionized water are used as PL quencher of N-CDarg. As shown in Fig. 5 a the maximum PL quenching (Io/I = 2.85) is observed for TC. A mild interference is shown by oxytetracycline (Io/I = 1.31), which is a structural analogue of tetracycline. A mild interference is revealed for other antibiotics such as penicillin, erythromycin, chloramphenicol, and amoxicillin, with Io/I in the range of 1.05 to 1.10. In real water analysis, it is relevant to assess the PL quenching by common metal ions and anions as control for detection of TC and 4-NP. It is further noted from Fig. 5b and 5c that the PL quenching of the probe by common cations (e.g., Na+, K+, Fe2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Cr3+, Mn2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Ni2+) and anions (e.g., Cl−, F−, SO4 2−, NO3 −, CO3 2−, HCO3 −, CH3COO−) is negligible (Io/I < 1.02). Mild interference is revealed by Cd2+ (Io/I = 1.08), Pb2+ (Io/I = 1.11) and Ni2+ (Io/I = 1.16). The above results indicate good selectivity of TC detection by N-CDarg probe over other common antibiotics as well as common cations and anions. In this study, the solutions of the interfering metal ions were prepared by the serial dilution of ICPMS standard stock solution of respective elements in deionized water. The solutions of anions were prepared from the respective sodium salts of the corresponding salt of anions.

Furthermore, the selectivity of 4-NP by N-CDarg is not interfered by the common cations (Fig. 5b) and common anions (Fig. 5c). Furthermore, Fig. 5b and 5c also demonstrates that detection of 4-NP mixed in de-ionized water was very selective against the above cations and anions. Fig. 5d demonstrates the comparison of PL quenching of 4-NP with respect to the structural analogues of 4-nitrophenol, e.g., 2,4-Dinitrophenol (DNP), 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (TNP), 3-Nitrotoulene (3-NT), 4-Chloroaniline (4-CA), 2-Nitroaniline (2-NA), 4-Nitrobenzoic acid (4-NBA), 2-Chlorophenol (2-CP), 1,3-Dinitrobenzene (1,3-DNB) and 4-Nitroaniline (4-NA). The concentration of all these potential quenchers is taken as 8 μM. It is noted that the PL quenching by 4-NP was maximum (Io/I = 1.51), as compared to the PL quenching by DNP (Io/I = 1.18) and by TNP (Io/I = 1.16). The PL quenching by the other potential quenchers are very low, i.e., Io/I = 1.03–1.08. The interference study suggests that the N-CDarg PL probe is highly selective towards detection of TC and 4-NP in the presence of wide range of interfering agents and hence N-CDarg can be used for real sample studies.

Detection of TC and 4-NP in real samples is investigated by spiking range of known concentrations of TC (1 to 60 μM) and 4-NP (1 to 10 μM), respectively in municipality tap water, urban river water. A good linear response in the range of 1 to 60 μM was observed (Fig. 6 a). Compared to the TC of similar concentration range spiked in de-ionized water, the corresponding sensitivity of TC detection in municipality tap water, urban river water is determined as 0.016 μM−1 and 0.007 μM−1, respectively. The corresponding LODs are calculated as 0.18 μM and 0.42 μM respectively. Similarly, a linear response is obtained for the different concentrations of 4-NP ranging between 1 and 10 μM spiked in municipality tap water, urban river water (Fig. 6b). The corresponding sensitivity are 0.045 μM−1 and 0.024 μM−1, respectively. The LoDs of 4-NP detection in municipality tap water, urban river water is 0.13 μM and 0.25 μM, respectively. Compared to the TC and 4-NP spiked in de-ionized water, its sensitivity as well as the LoDs are decreased in the real samples owing to matrix effect. The recovery analysis, i.e., estimation of TC and 4-NP concentration spiked in municipal tap water and urban river water as compared to the spiked concentration is listed in Table S2a and Table S2b (Supporting Information) respectively. Notably, the recovery analysis of TC was poor (about 20 % higher) for very low spiked TC concentration (1 μM). For higher spiked concentration, the recovery analysis of TC was within 4.5 %. On the other hand, the recovery analysis of 4-NP was between 8 and 12 % only at lower spiked concentration.

Further TC detection in real milk sample by N-CDarg was investigated by spike analysis method. The commercially procured milk was diluted by ten times. Batches of the diluted milk sample were spiked with TC whose concentration ranged between 1 and 60 μM. These batches of spiked samples were then treated with TCA for removing the matrix of the milk composition [34]. Then each batch was treated with the probe solution as discussed earlier. The PL quenching plot for each spiked concentration is given in Fig. S5, Supporting Information. A linear response is obtained, and the sensitivity obtained from the slope of the plot is 0.005 μM−1 (Fig. 6c). The LoD of TC detection in milk sample is determined as of 0.60 μM. The results of recovery analysis given in Table S3 (Supporting Information), suggests that the error in such measurement is less than 10 %. The LOD of TC in milk sample i.e. 0.60 μM (600 nM) is within the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (USFDA) specified range of the maximum residual limits (MRLs) of tetracycline in milk, i.e., 676 nM [35]. In case of 4-NP the obtained LODs 0.077 μM (in DI water), 0.13 μM (in municipality tap water) and 0.25 μM (in urban river water) are also lesser than the 4-NP in drinking water or ground water specified by the US Environmental Protection Agency (60 ng/mL, or 0.43 μM) [36]. Table S4, (Supporting Information) demonstrates the comparison of LODs of different probes for the detection of TC and 4-NP; and it is observed that our probe demonstrates comparable sensitivity and selectivity for detection of TC and 4-NP. However, the LODs of different ions and molecules reported in literature are also compared with the LODs obtained for TC and 4-NP in present study (Table S5, Supporting Information).

The comparison of PL lifetime of probe and probe treated with quencher is important to understand the PL quenching mechanism. The PL decay and the UV–vis spectra of the probe (only N-CDarg) and the probe treated with different concentrations of TC and 4-NP, respectively are given in Fig. S6a to S6d (Supporting Information). The PL decay spectra for both the systems are best fitted with three lifetime components, and the fitted values are given in Table 1a, 1b
. The ratio of the average PL lifetime of only N-CDarg probe and probe treated with analyte (i.e., τ0/τ) is about unity for the batch treated by TC and 4-NP. This eliminates the possibility of PL quenching via fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) and by charge transfer based dynamic quenching mechanism, rather it confers to static quenching due ground state complexation [37,38]. The ground state complex formation is likely to occur due to the interaction between the electron deficient quencher and the lone pair of electrons on nitrogen atom of N-CDarg [39]. The formation of ground state complexation is corroborated by the minor shift in the absorbance peak corresponding to the n-π* transition in TC and in 4-NP (Fig. S6b and S6d, Supporting Information). The proposed phenomenon of the ground state complexation between the probe and the quenchers (TC and 4-NP) is also reflected from the red shift in their respective PL spectra (Fig. 4a and c). In addition, the PL quenching can occur due to inner filter effect (IFE), attributed to spectral overlap between UV–vis absorption spectrum of the analyte (i.e., TC and 4-NP) with the excitation and emission spectra of probe (N-CDarg) in Supporting Information (Fig. S7a, S7b).

Sub-10 nm sized N-CDarg photoluminescent probe is successfully synthesized by thermal conjugation method by heating 1,4-Butanediol with L-Arginine monohydrochloride at 180 °C. The thoroughly characterized water soluble N-CDarg is exhibited high photoluminescence quantum yield and displayed efficient photoluminescence quenching with tetracycline and 4-nitrophenol, which are considered as emerging and persistent organic pollutants in water. The PL quenching is dependent on the concentrations of tetracycline and 4-nitrophenol, where a linear PL quenching is obtained for concentration range of 0.50 to 60 μM for tetracycline 0.4 to 20 μM for 4-nitrophenol. The PL quenching mechanism has been studied by comparing the steady state as well as the PL decay lifetime spectroscopy of probe and probe treated with analyte, which suggested ground state complexation as the predominant phenomena leading to PL quenching. The sensing application of N-CDarg as a PL probe is substantiated by selective PL quenching against wide range of metal ions, anions, antibiotics and structural analogues of phenols and nitrophenols as background species. The real time application of N-CDarg as a PL probe has been demonstrated by determining limit of detection of tetracycline and 4-nitrophenol, and their spike recovery analysis in real sample matrices, e.g., municipality water, urban river water and milk.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: KB is grateful to IIT Roorkee for providing a Senior Research Fellowship. The authors acknowledge DST-FIST, New Delhi (SR/FST/CS-II/2018/72) for funding 500 MHz NMR facility in the Department of Chemistry, IIT Roorkee. The authors are thankful to Prof. B.P. Vellanki, Civil Engineering Department of IIT Roorkee for MS-QToF facility. The authors also acknowledge Institute Instrumentation Centre of IIT Roorkee for the XPS and FLS facility, and Department of Metallurgical and Materials Engineering, IIT Roorkee for 300 kV HRTEM facility.
